summary: O'Neil debunks the common argument that people willingly trade their private data for internet services, making it a fair exchange that doesn't require regulation. She argues this framing is a fallacy because users have no real understanding of what they're trading away—they don't know about data aggregation, re-identification techniques, or how their information will be used for credit and employment decisions years later. The 'trade' is actually a trick that exploits human short-sightedness and addiction to immediate gratification. Clicking 'yes' on a privacy form is not informed consent and should not be treated as a political statement against privacy regulation.
key_points: The 'informed market participant' model completely breaks down when applied to data privacy—users lack basic knowledge about data brokers, re-identification, and downstream uses
Data given away today has future consequences users aren't considering, from credit decisions to employment screening
The asymmetry of information between tech companies and users makes this exchange fundamentally unfair, not a legitimate trade
Clicking 'yes' on privacy forms represents impulse and ignorance, not meaningful consent or a political stance against regulation
money_quotes: The real problem is that people generally have no clue what it is they're trading.

This idea of a omniscient market participant with perfect information making a well-informed trade, which we've already seen is not the case in the actual market, is doubly or triply not the case when you think about young people giving away private data for the sake of a phone app.
People are ignorant of what they're giving away to play Candy Crush Saga.
It's a trick which plays on our own impulses and short-sightedness and possibly even a kind of addiction to shiny toys in the form of candy.
If you give me your future, I'll give you a shiny toy to play with right now.

People who click 'yes' are not signaling that they've thought deeply about the consequences of giving their data away, and they are certainly not making the definitive political statement that we don't need privacy regulation.
key_insight: The data-for-services 'trade' is not a fair market exchange but a trick exploiting information asymmetry and human short-sightedness, and should not be used to argue against privacy regulation.
